critique.summary: Peter Vandermeersch, a senior journalist at Mediahuis, was suspended after admitting to using AI tools to generate inaccurate quotes in his Substack newsletter. An internal investigation by Mediahuis's NRC newspaper found multiple false quotes attributed to individuals who denied making them.
mainPoints: Peter Vandermeersch used AI tools like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google’s NotebookLM to summarize reports and generate quotes without verifying their accuracy.
He admitted to 'wrongly putting words into people’s mouths' and acknowledged the mistake of not applying necessary human oversight.
Mediahuis suspended Vandermeersch and removed several of his articles from the Irish Independent website.
The investigation was conducted by NRC, a Mediahuis title where Vandermeersch was formerly editor-in-chief, which found dozens of false quotes.
Vandermeersch expressed regret and emphasized that journalism requires human work despite AI’s potential as a tool.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with publicly available information from reputable sources, including direct statements from Peter Vandermeersch and Mediahuis executives. The investigation by NRC, a known Mediahuis publication, supports the allegations of AI-generated false quotes. Vandermeersch's own admission and Mediahuis's response confirm the accuracy of the reported events. The article provides a balanced account with multiple perspectives, including the journalist's explanation and the publisher's stance.
considerations: The article does not discuss broader implications for AI use in journalism beyond this case, such as industry-wide guidelines or ethical standards.
There is no mention of any legal consequences or further disciplinary actions beyond suspension.
The potential impact on readers' trust in AI-assisted journalism is not explored.
The article does not provide detailed examples of the false quotes or the specific nature of the inaccuracies.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a journalist suspended for using AI-generated quotes, with commenters debating the responsibility of the journalist, the role of editorial oversight, and the broader implications of AI in journalism. Many express concern about blind trust in AI and the erosion of journalistic standards, while some highlight systemic issues in media economics and the emotional manipulation inherent in AI tools.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: The tool didn't fail here, the person did. An experienced journalist should know better. Editorial review exists for exactly this reason, if you skip it, this is what happens.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately assigns responsibility to the journalist and emphasizes the importance of editorial oversight, which aligns with standard journalistic practices.

comments.author: mmooss
comments.text:
They said earlier that they didn't verify the quotes. I understand them to mean that the LLM outputted text that included quotes. They assumed the output was accurate and found it so appealing, on an emotional level, that they just went with it without checking.

The most valuable lesson here, by far, is not about other people but about ourselves. This person is trained, takes it seriously, and advocates for making sure the AI is supervised, and got caught in the emotional manipulation of LLM design [0].

We all are at risk. If we look at the other person and mock them, and think we are better than them, we are only exposing ourselves to more risk. If we think - oh my goodness, look what happened, this is perilous - then we gain from what happened and can protect ourselves.

(We might also ask why this valuable tool also includes such manipulative interface. Don't take it for granted; it's not at all necessary for LLMs to work, and they could just as easily sound like a-holes.)

[0] I mean that obviously they are carefully designed to sound appealing
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully discusses the emotional appeal of AI-generated content and the universal risk of being misled, providing a nuanced perspective on AI's influence.

comments.author: crop_rotation
comments.text: HN is full of people saying ABCD should know better and honestly I thought the same, but when I look at almost all of my friends working in critical domains like as a judge or engineer or lawyer or even doctor, they seem to trust ChatGPT more or less blindly. People get defensive when I point out out to them that ChatGPT will make things up and it is widely know, and some even tell me it is the fault of "tech people" for not fixing it and they can't be expected to double check every chatgpt conversation. So I am very sure this problem is more prevalent than what we see and also that it is going to continue increasing.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a widespread issue of overreliance on AI without verification, which is a relevant concern in many professional domains.

comments.author: abaieorro
comments.text:
> I wrongly put words into people’s mouths, when I should have presented them as paraphrases

Journalists were doing this for decades. Stitching and editing words out of context, to put words into peoples mouths! I will take AI halucinations over journalists halucinations anytime, at least machine has no hostile intent, and is making a geunine error!
comments.analysis: The comment provides historical context on journalistic practices, suggesting AI errors may be less malicious than human editorial bias, though it may downplay the seriousness of AI misinformation.

comments.author: phreack
comments.text:
> “It is particularly painful that I made precisely the mistake I have repeatedly warned colleagues about: these language models are so good that they produce irresistible quotes you are tempted to use as an author. Of course, I should have verified them. The necessary ‘human oversight’, which I consistently advocate, fell short.”

What? Irresistible quotes? This betrays a terrible way of thinking as a journalist. Basically an admission of wanting to fake news that'd sound good. At that point just write fiction.
comments.analysis: This comment critically addresses the ethical lapse implied by the journalist's admission, highlighting the danger of prioritizing appealing content over accuracy.

comments.author: intended
comments.text:
Looking at the media ecosystem at large, gives me a case of gallows humor.

In some sections of the ecosystem, firms still penalize journalists for errors. In other sections, checking reduces the velocity of attention grabbing headlines. The difference in treatment is… farcical.

We need more good journalists, and more good journalism - but we no longer have ways to subsidize such work. Ads / classifieds are dead, and revenue accrues to only a few.

I have no idea how we square this circle.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully contextualizes the problem within the economic challenges facing journalism, explaining systemic pressures that may contribute to such incidents.

comments.author: PeterStuer
comments.text: "Journalism" over here seems to have died a long time ago. Most if not all of the former "quality newspapers" unfortunately seem to have devolved into what could be more accurately described as "pro regime activist blogs".
comments.analysis: This comment is highly emotional and presents a sweeping generalization without evidence, reducing its credibility and constructive value.

comments.author: maxrmk
comments.text:
Ironic coming from the Guardian. One of their journalists consistently publishes ai slop and the paper is in denial about it.

https://x.com/maxwelltani/status/2023089526445371777?s=46
comments.analysis: This comment calls out perceived hypocrisy, which may be valid but lacks detailed evidence here and leans toward emotional criticism.

comments.author: Chinjut
comments.text:
Good lord, even the apology is AI generated: "That was not just careless—it was wrong."

https://pressanddemocracy.substack.com/p/i-am-admitting-my-m...
comments.analysis: This comment appears to mock the apology, suggesting it was AI-generated, but offers no substantive analysis and leans toward trolling.

comments.author: shahbaby
comments.text:
> That was not just careless – it was wrong

lol
comments.analysis: This brief comment dismisses the apology with a 'lol', contributing little to the discussion and serving mostly as a dismissive reaction.

comments.author: camillomiller
comments.text: I have witnessed in person what LLMs have done to the mind of seemingly intelligent people. It’s a disaster.
comments.analysis: While emotional, this comment reflects a common concern about AI's impact on critical thinking, though it lacks specific examples or evidence.
